w:r.w:t: FYP Platform for Final Year Students

The primary stakeholders for this system are the final-year (senior) students, as they are most directly affected by the FYP process. Secondary stakeholders are project supervisors and the FYP Coordinator.
Requirements were gathered through informal discussions with senior students
and then having an in-depth discussion
To avoid gold plating (adding features that
focused strictly on solving real, reported problems
elicited requirements were then separated into functional requirements and non-functional
The following problems came up consistently across discussions with senior students and
then going through some research online based on
through review of the existing process:
Students pick topics without knowing if the same project was done before.
Supervisors sometimes become unreachable after the
Group contribution is uneven, with no way to measure individual effort.
Progress is not tracked regularly, leading to last-minute failures.
Students have no formal way to report a
Users log in and see a dashboard specific to their role: student, supervisor, or admin. No user can access another
Students add their technical skills to their
. Supervisors add their research areas. This information is visible to both sides before a supervision request is made.
profile shows their average response time to student submissions. Students can view this before choosing a supervisor.
Students form a project group of up to three members. Each member is linked to the shared project, and individual contributions are logged separately.
Students can post a listing to find group members with specific skills they need for their project.
Supervisors can post project ideas to an Idea B
. Students who do not yet have a topic can browse and select an idea from this bank.
their project title, domain, and proposal details for supervisor review.
, the system checks whether the same title or
project already exists in the archive. If a match is found, the submission is blocked and the student is shown a message explaining why.
Groups send a formal supervision request to a specific teacher through the system.
Supervisors accept or decline supervision requests and must provide a written reason for any rejection.
Supervisors define deadlines for major project phases such as
-defense and final submission. These deadlines are visible to all group members.
Students upload project files at any stage of the project. The system keeps a record of all
versions so that earlier files can still be retrieved.
a short weekly update log describing what they worked on.
Supervisors write feedback on uploaded files and weekly logs. Feedback is saved with the date and time it was written.
any progress log within a period defined by the coordinator, the system automatically sends an alert to both the group and their supervisor.
each other's individual contributions. These ratings are kept hidden from other group members. Only the supervisor and admin can view the aggregated results.
Supervisors enter final marks through the system using a grading rubric. The rubric criteria are visible to students from the beginning of the semester.
Completed projects are saved in a searchable archive so that junior students can browse them as examples of past work.
a formal complaint to the admin if their supervisor is unresponsive or they have an unresolved issue.
within an acceptable time under normal usage. This threshold will be agreed upon with the IT department during the design phase.
Passwords must be stored using a secure hashing method. Student grades and peer review scores must only be visible to users with the
The system must remain operational throughout the active academic year, especially during high-traffic
such as the final submission week.
a server failure, uploaded project files must be recoverable up to the most recent backup point.
A new student must be able to complete the proposal submission process on their first login without needing a tutorial or external help.
The system must work correctly on current versions of Chrome and Firefox and must be usable on standard mobile screen sizes.
The system must support all registered final-year students and supervisors using it at the same time without any slowdown.
The code must follow a modular structure so that a developer familiar with the codebase can
and fix a defect in a reasonable amount of time.
by group members must be completely hidden from all other group members. Only the supervisor and admin can view the aggregated results.
Text and background colors must have sufficient contrast so that students with low vision can read the interface without difficulty.
below illustrates the system boundary, the three primary actors
The following tables describe the core use cases in detail following the standard IBM tabular format.
Student submits a new project proposal including title, domain, and abstract for approval.
3. System checks for a duplicate title «include».
3a. A matching title is found → System blocks submission and asks
The duplicate check algorithm must respond in under 2 seconds.
is logged in, a proposal has been
, and the chosen supervisor has not reached their group limit.
browses supervisor profiles and selects one.
links the group to the supervisor.
3a. Supervisor rejects with written reason → Student notified, can request another supervisor.
-defined period → Admin is alerted to assign manually.
is logged in and has an active, supervisor-approved project.
saves the file and assigns a version number.
receives a notification of the new upload.
3a. File type is not supported → System rejects upload and tells student which types are accepted.
the peer review window has been opened by the coordinator.
rates each group member using the provided rubric.
saves ratings as private and confirms submission.
3a. Form is incomplete → System highlights missing fields and prevents submission.
privately. Not visible to other group members.
